name: Gravel through Penedès, the capital of cava
name_original: Gravel por el Penedès, la capital del cava
description:
Sant Sadurní d'Anoia is known as the capital of cava, for its manufacturing tradition and for bringing together in its municipal district some of the largest cavas in the region and throughout the state. This Route passes through some of these caves, but first it will go through the foothills of the Serra de l'Ordal, to gain some height and be able to contemplate this part of the Penedès, which geographically and in terms of landscape is integrated into the so-called Valls de l'Anoia. On the way back, it goes up parallel to the Bitlles River to cross the northern part of the Penedès plain.

The La Granada train station is the beginning of this route, which quickly takes you to the small town of Sant Sebastià dels Gorgs, where there is an interesting Romanesque monastery. The first ramps go up to Miravinya La Bardera, one of the five themed viewpoints scattered throughout the Penedès. From there you can see your next destination, Sant Sadurní d'Anoia, which you reach after a quick descent along a dirt track, be careful with the tight curves with loose sand!

After crossing a level crossing with a barrier, you will arrive at Sant Sadurní, leaving the Freixenet cellars on the right. After crossing Sant Sadurní, the route crosses the Anoia river, but first you will pass through the cellar, and the oldest company in the country, the Codorníu cellars, founded in 1551! The cellar building is modernist in style, the work of Puig i Cadafalch.

Shortly after, the Anoia river is crossed for the first time, it is a pass with little flow, but it is possible that your feet get a little wet. On one side there are cement pylons that allow safe passage if the flow is greater. After crossing the center of Can Catasús and a brief incursion by road, you will cross the Anoia river again, this time over a narrow bridge, almost a footbridge, very particular.

You now enter a different landscape, of small valleys, marked by the presence of the Anoia River and its tributary, the Bitlles River. In this area of the Valls d'Anoia you will find old paper mills, although few retain their original function. Heading towards Sant Pere de Riudebitlles you will see some. The section from Torrelavit to Sant Pere is one of my favorites on the Route: the Camí de les Hortes, true to its name, surrounded by orchards, and passing by a spectacular restored mill, the Molí Blanc, now a rural hotel.

After crossing Sant Pere, a short ascent begins to another Miravinya, that of La Cadira, with a good perspective of the Penedès plain. Located on a small mound, it can be cycled to the top, although it may be better to push the bike depending on the tires and/or gear. After a new section between vineyards, you reach another viewpoint, the Creu de Lavit, to say goodbye to the Valls de Mediona and definitively return to the flat.

On the way to Pla del Penedès, next to the Can Bas wineries, is the small church of Sant Joan Salerm, one of my favorite images of the Penedès. You will enter the Pla through the small torrent de la Rovira, which is crossed by a somewhat precarious plank, I recommend dismounting. After leaving the town, you cross an extensive succession of vineyards to Granada, where the route passes by the remains of its 10th-century castle, before returning to the train station, closing the route.
region_intro: Gravel biking around La Granada offers routes through the Penedès wine region, characterized by rolling hills, vineyards, and agricultural plains. The terrain often includes a mix of unpaved roads and tracks, with some sections providing elevated views. The area features varied elevations, ranging from lower plains to higher points within the Penedès landscape. Best gravel bike trails around La Granada The most popular gravel bike trail is The south of the plain in gravel, a 42.4 miles (68.2 km) trail that takes 5 hours 22 minutes to complete, traversing open plains and rural landscapes. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Gravel through Penedès, the capital of cava, a difficult 32.8 miles (52.7 km) path. This route winds through the renowned Penedès wine region, offering views of vineyards and local towns. Local gravel bikers also love the Gravel from the balcony of Penedès, a 36.1 miles (58.2 km) trail leading through elevated sections of the Penedès region, often completed in about 5 hours 10 minutes. Gravel biking around La Granada is defined by vineyard-lined paths, rolling hills, and rural tracks, with options available for both moderate and challenging ability levels. The routes in La Granada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 500 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore La Granada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many gravel bike trails are available in La Granada?
A: La Granada offers a wide selection of gravel biking adventures, with nearly 100 routes available on komoot. These routes cater to various skill levels, from moderate to challenging.

Q: Are there any easy gravel bike trails suitable for beginners in La Granada?
A: While many routes in La Granada are rated moderate to difficult, the region's terrain of rolling hills and vineyard-lined paths can be enjoyed by beginners by choosing shorter sections or less elevated routes. Currently, komoot lists 29 moderate routes that might be more accessible for those new to gravel biking.

Q: What are some of the more challenging gravel bike routes around La Granada?
A: For experienced gravel bikers seeking a challenge, routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e840866966/gravel-to-the-highest-of-penedes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel to the highest of Penedes&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gains and longer distances. Another demanding option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e840487889/gravel-with-sea-views-in-penedes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel with sea views in Penedes&lt;/a&gt;, which provides both a workout and stunning vistas.

Q: Can I find circular gravel bike routes in La Granada?
A: Yes, most gravel bike routes in La Granada are designed as circular tours, allowing you to start and end at the same point. This is common for exploring the Penedès wine region, making logistics easier for riders.

Q: What is the typical length and duration of gravel bike trails in La Granada?
A: Gravel bike trails in La Granada vary in length, with many routes ranging from 30 to over 80 kilometers. For example, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e835825495/the-south-of-the-plain-in-gravel" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The south of the plain in gravel&lt;/a&gt; is 68.2 km and takes over 5 hours, while &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e836398794/gravel-from-the-balcony-of-penedes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel from the balcony of Penedès&lt;/a&gt; is 58.2 km and takes around 5 hours 10 minutes. Durations depend on your pace and stops.

Q: Are there gravel bike routes with scenic viewpoints in La Granada?
A: Absolutely. The Penedès region is known for its picturesque landscapes. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e836398794/gravel-from-the-balcony-of-penedes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel from the balcony of Penedès&lt;/a&gt; lead through elevated sections, offering expansive views of the vineyards and rolling hills. You can also find routes with distant sea views, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e840487889/gravel-with-sea-views-in-penedes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel with sea views in Penedes&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What kind of attractions can I see along the gravel bike trails in La Granada?
A: The region around La Granada is rich in history and natural beauty. You can explore historical sites and castles like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4418293" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of La Granada&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3922796" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olèrdola Castle and Archaeological Site&lt;/a&gt;. Other points of interest include religious buildings such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3980630" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Olesa de Bonesvalls&lt;/a&gt; and natural spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3312743" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gran Pèlag of Vilobí del Penedès&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is La Granada a good destination for family-friendly gravel biking?
A: While many routes are rated moderate to difficult, the Penedès region offers a mix of terrain. Families might enjoy exploring flatter sections through vineyards or shorter, less elevated routes. It's advisable to check the specific route profiles for elevation and distance to ensure it's suitable for all family members.

Q: What do other gravel bikers say about the trails in La Granada?
A: The gravel biking routes in La Granada are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an average rating of 4.5 stars from over 50 reviews. Riders frequently praise the beautiful vineyard landscapes, the mix of unpaved roads, and the opportunity to explore the Penedès wine region.

Q: When is the best time of year to go gravel biking in La Granada?
A: The Penedès region generally offers pleasant conditions for gravel biking for much of the year. Spring and autumn provide mild temperatures and beautiful scenery, especially during harvest season. Summer can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended. Winter can also be suitable, though some days might be cooler or wetter.

Q: Are there gravel bike routes that pass by cafes or places to refuel?
A: Given La Granada's location in the Penedès wine region, many routes will naturally pass through or near small towns and villages where you can find local cafes, wineries, and restaurants to refuel and experience local gastronomy. Planning your route with komoot allows you to identify such points of interest along the way.

Q: How does gravel biking in La Granada compare to nearby towns?
A: La Granada is centrally located within the Penedès wine region, offering a quintessential experience of vineyard-lined gravel paths and rolling hills. While nearby towns might offer similar terrain, La Granada provides direct access to the heart of Cava country, making it an excellent base for exploring the unique agricultural and historical landscape of this specific area.
tips: Romanesque monastery founded in 1030 in the old pre-Romanesque chapel of Els Gorgs, to which a bell tower was added during its reform. Between 1981 and 1977 the cloister was restored, recovering its original appearance, and in a second phase, between 2000 and 2002, the bell tower was restored. A third restoration between 2010 and 2011 protected the cloister with a new cover.

Viewpoint that is part of the five Miravinya, is located next to the La Bardera farmhouse (S. XIV), one of the oldest in Subirats. You can also see the monument in homage to the peasantry and winegrowers of Penedès, the work of the artist Rufino Mesa. From the viewpoint you can see the northern part of the Penedès plain.

As long as the water does not exceed the level of the cement pylons that run through the pass, it is safe to cross it. In any case, and except in case of heavy rain, the flow is not excessive and the worst that can happen to us is that we get our feet a little wet.
It is a narrow concrete bridge, but wide enough for a bicycle to pass, with a metal railing. Connect the road to Can Catasús with the Bitlles road to Sant Pere de Riudebitlles.

A giant chair stands on the small hill of Malgranada (Torrelavit), an ideal place from which to interpret nature, with a 360-degree view. The chair invites you to stop and contemplate, to sit and listen in the middle of a privileged landscape, the Penedés.

The cross marks the boundary between the municipalities of Torrelavit and Pla del Penedès, it has been recorded since the 18th century and has undergone several reforms and was even moved for a time to the Lavit rectory until it was returned to its original location in 1981. From the site you can see the Bitlles river valley and the Montserrat mountain in the background.

The church of Sant Joan Salerm is in the Romanesque style (10th century), with a bell tower added in the 18th century. As a curiosity, the etymology of "salerm" refers to "uncultivated land", since at the time of its construction, long before vineyards began to be cultivated in the Penedès, this place was a forest.

The passage of this small torrent is saved with a precarious metal plate, it is not very difficult, but after crossing it, the path makes a sharp turn to the right and as a precaution, I recommend dismounting from the bicycle and walking.

The remains of this fortification date from the 10th century, when the municipality was created, and it has been the protagonist of various historical episodes: its destruction in 1003 after a Saracen attack, the siege in the 15th century during the Catalan civil war, or in the XVII, during the War of the Segadors, in which the French troops defeated the Castilians in this same place. It was from this fact that a process of plundering began that lasted for centuries. The rectangular tower of the castle is not preserved and only a few walls remain.

Stepping-stone crossing over the Anoia River
